body.u: lot job-Give it up-dole-looking around-love/marriage-kids?-divorce (maybe)-restlessness-middle classness-contentment
In a way I was glad to get out of school. The longer I stayed the more unbearable it became. Four ‘O’level equivalents was enough for me. I left.

I remember there was a lot of friction in the family over my decision. Parents wanted me to stay on and get ‘A’s. But I stuck to my guns as a gesture to my parents/teachers/myself, that I was my own boss. Although, if getting a job had been left to me I would have been on the dole all my life.

Mum found me a nice office job. I took it without a word of protest. Jobs were scarce, it was the done thing to take whatever came. Parents were glad. They had married me off to the system and I said ‘I do’ without giving it a second thought. One morning when I was 18 I looked in the mirror to see I was turning into a nice office person, so I chucked the job in. I divorced the system, for a little while any way. The years from 18-23 were bad for me. I was looking around for something that couldn’t be found. I had dropped out and it was hard because I had never repelled before. Parents were worried, even shocked. I wasn’t but I got depressed a lot. There were many different paths ahead of me. I didn’t want to take one. Social life consisted of drinking, pictures and football. I wasn’t going anywhere fast.
After a few years of this I knew myself a bit better. The rebel in me had died. I got myself a steady job which was bearable and paid well.
I hadn’t been bothered with girls before but when love hit me it got me hard. I left my parents to live with her. It was glorious. She was an angel. I was happy.

Then things lost their originality. Marriage came, kids came (two one boy, one girl) By thirty-three. I was in the same trap as my father was at that age and probably his father before him. At thirty five I changed jobs and went up in the world dramatically. It didn’t make me happier but she liked it (wife). A forty I can safely say my life had ended. It was too late to start again. I wasn’t content but I just had to accept it.

As I began to approach retirement I became less worried about happiness. I was able to accept what I had an enjoy it. I had a lovely wife, kids (now all grown up) and a comfortable house. I enjoyed the simple pleasures in life and stopped looking around for something more.
Looking back now I think somewhere I made the wrong decision I should have gone round the world or something. Now I smile at my grandchildren and wait for death.
